DOMAINS: Longevity, Health, Fitness, Nutrition, Metabolism, Strength Training, Sleep
NOTABLE WORKS: Outlive: The Science and Art of Longevity, The Drive Podcast, Early and Often (newsletter). Medical degree (Stanford), surgical training (Johns Hopkins).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Physician with deep expertise in longevity medicine. Outlive is excellent on the 'four horsemen' (heart disease, cancer, neurodegenerative disease, metabolic dysfunction) and is directly referenced in the Body and Fuel phases. Some commercial interests (membership model, supplements). Podcast guests are of variable quality — use the book and core frameworks, not every guest episode. Somewhat troublesome politically; best used as inspiration rather than quoted by name.

DOMAINS: Empathy, Vulnerability, Emotional Intelligence, Coaching, Leadership, Shame Resilience
NOTABLE WORKS: Daring Greatly, The Gifts of Imperfection, Rising Strong, Braving the Wilderness, Dare to Lead, Atlas of the Heart, I Thought It Was Just Me. Published Research (University of Houston). Multiple TED talks (60M+ combined views).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Research professor of social work. Twenty years of qualitative research on vulnerability, courage, shame, and empathy. Referenced throughout the program — Week 1 (Power of Vulnerability TED talk), Week 6 (rumble framework, Atlas of the Heart), Week 11 (self-compassion), Weeks 1 & 15 (Dare to Lead values exercise). Core to the program's emotional foundation. No significant cautions — widely respected across academic and applied settings.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Coaching Habit, The Advice Trap, How To Begin, How To Work With (Almost) Anyone. Founder of Box of Crayons (coaching training company). Rhodes Scholar.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: The Coaching Habit is the best-selling coaching book of the decade — practical, concise, and directly applicable to the peer coaching model in this program. The seven essential questions framework ('And what else?', 'What's the real challenge here for you?') maps directly to Weeks 5–8 coaching skill progression. The Advice Trap deepens the 'stay curious longer' discipline. Respected across ICF, corporate, and education coaching communities. No significant cautions.

DOMAINS: Coaching Standards, Ethics, Competency Frameworks, Professional Development
NOTABLE WORKS: ICF Code of Ethics, ICF Core Competencies (8 competencies, updated 2019), ICF Credentialing Framework (ACC, PCC, MCC). Global governing body for professional coaching.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: The global standard-setting body for professional coaching with 60,000+ members worldwide. ICF Core Competencies are the backbone of the coaching skill progression throughout this program (Weeks 1–16). The Code of Ethics underpins Week 1's scope-of-practice discussion (coaching vs. therapy vs. training). Non-negotiable reference for any coaching-based program. No cautions.

DOMAINS: Organisational Psychology, Motivation, Rethinking, Generosity, Potential
NOTABLE WORKS: Think Again, Originals, Give and Take, Hidden Potential, Option B (with Sheryl Sandberg). Published Research (Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania). WorkLife Podcast.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Youngest tenured professor at Wharton. Prolific researcher with genuine academic credentials in organisational psychology. Think Again is relevant to growth mindset and challenging assumptions (Weeks 2, 4). Give and Take connects to coaching culture and generosity in group dynamics. Hidden Potential aligns with the 'Do What You Can' philosophy — progress over perfection. Strong communicator who stays close to the evidence. No significant cautions.

NOTABLE WORKS: Good Habits, Bad Habits: The Science of Making Positive Changes That Stick. Published Research (University of Southern California). Provost Professor of Psychology and Business.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: One of the world's leading academic researchers on habit formation. Thirty years of published peer-reviewed research on automaticity and behaviour change. Good Habits, Bad Habits is the most research-grounded book in the habits space — more rigorous than Clear (Atomic Habits) on the underlying science. Directly relevant to Week 3 (habit loop, environment design, friction management). Complementary to Clear and Fogg — Wood provides the deepest academic foundation.

DOMAINS: Habits, Behaviour Change, Identity, Systems Design
NOTABLE WORKS: Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones. 3-2-1 Newsletter (2M+ subscribers). jamesclear.com.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Excellent synthesiser and communicator, not a primary researcher. Atomic Habits is one of the best-selling non-fiction books of the decade and is directly referenced in Week 3 (habit stacking). The identity-based habit framework ('I'm becoming someone who...') is also relevant to Week 15's identity and values work. Clear's strength is accessibility — use alongside Wood (academic depth) and Fogg (behaviour design). His habit stacking video is a Week 3 home task.

DOMAINS: Evolutionary Biology, Anthropology, Human Movement, Biomechanics, Evolutionary Mismatch
NOTABLE WORKS: Exercised: Why Something We Never Evolved to Do Is Healthy and Rewarding, The Story of the Human Body: Evolution, Health, and Disease. Published Research (Harvard University). Professor of Human Evolutionary Biology.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Harvard professor and one of the foremost authorities on why humans evolved to move — and what happens when we don't. Directly referenced in Week 7 (evolutionary context for movement), Week 9 (aerobic fitness), and Week 12 (evolutionary mismatch and nutrition). His central argument — that we evolved for regular, varied physical activity, not for exercise — is foundational to the program's 'all movement is good' philosophy. Rigorous, evidence-based, no significant cautions.

DOMAINS: Generalism, Learning, Talent Development, Sport Science
NOTABLE WORKS: Range: Why Generalists Triumph in a Specialized World, The Sports Gene: Inside the Science of Extraordinary Athletic Performance. Investigative journalist (ProPublica).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Investigative journalist, not a researcher. Range makes a compelling case for broad experience over narrow specialisation — useful counterbalance to the performative culture that many participants may come from. The Sports Gene is relevant background for dispelling fitness myths. A useful book for considering different approaches to life, but not a core reference for the program's primary domains.

DOMAINS: Nutrition, Supplementation, Longevity, Micronutrients, Sauna and Cold Exposure
NOTABLE WORKS: FoundMyFitness Podcast and Platform. PhD in Biomedical Science (University of Tennessee). Postdoctoral research (Salk Institute, with Bruce Ames).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Legitimate PhD researcher with genuine credentials in micronutrients and ageing. Excellent on vitamin D, omega-3s, and sauna research. Often too much focus on mechanisms over outcomes — deep biochemistry that may not translate directly to practical recommendations for general populations. Best used as background science for the facilitator rather than participant-facing content. Some commercial interests (supplement recommendations).

DOMAINS: Nutrition, Resistance Training, Evidence-Based Fitness, Body Composition
NOTABLE WORKS: Instagram @biolayne, Biolayne Podcast, Carbon Diet Coach (app). PhD in Nutritional Sciences (University of Illinois). Published Research. Champion powerlifter and natural bodybuilder.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Rigorous scientist with a genuine PhD and published research in nutritional sciences. Excellent at debunking fitness and nutrition misinformation. However, often controversial and unpalatable in delivery — combative social media presence that may alienate participants. Occasionally lets opinion sway advice. Best used as a background fact-checking resource for the facilitator rather than a participant-facing source. Relevant to Weeks 8 (strength) and 12–13 (nutrition).

DOMAINS: Exercise Physiology, Resistance Training, Aerobic Training, VO2 Max, Bioenergetics, Longevity, Sleep, Performance
NOTABLE WORKS: PERFORM Podcast, Perform with Dr. Andy Galpin (YouTube), Published Research (California State University, Fullerton). PhD in Human Bioenergetics. Huberman Lab guest series on fitness. Co-author of Unplugged.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Professor of Kinesiology with genuine published research in muscle physiology, bioenergetics, and exercise science. One of the most accessible and rigorous voices in exercise science. His Huberman Lab guest series on fitness is an excellent deep-dive. Directly relevant to Weeks 7–9 (movement, strength, aerobic fitness) and Week 10 (recovery). Rare combination of genuine academic depth and exceptional communication ability. No significant cautions — one of the safest and most credible sources on the list.

DOMAINS: Growth Mindset, Psychology, Motivation, Education, Personal Development
NOTABLE WORKS: Mindset: The New Psychology of Success (updated edition). Published Research (Stanford University). Lewis and Virginia Eaton Professor of Psychology.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Stanford professor and originator of growth mindset theory — one of the most influential ideas in modern psychology and education. Directly referenced in Week 2 (motivation) and woven throughout the program's philosophy. The core distinction (fixed vs. growth mindset) is well-validated. Caution: Dweck herself has acknowledged that growth mindset interventions in schools have shown smaller effects than initially hoped, and the concept is sometimes oversimplified in popular use. Use with nuance — it's a lens, not a magic switch.

NOTABLE WORKS: Start With Why, Leaders Eat Last, The Infinite Game, Find Your Why, Together Is Better. TED talk (65M+ views, third most watched ever).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Exceptional communicator and thought leader on purpose-driven leadership. Start With Why ('People don't buy what you do, they buy why you do it') connects to intrinsic motivation (Week 2) and values work (Weeks 1 & 15). Leaders Eat Last is relevant to group dynamics and psychological safety. Not a researcher — draws on existing research and historical examples to build frameworks. Strong on inspiration, occasionally light on nuance. Best used for motivational framing rather than scientific authority.

DOMAINS: Women's Health, Menopause, Resistance Training, Supplementation, Body Composition, Exercise Science
NOTABLE WORKS: Instagram @laurencs, Published Research (McMaster University, studying under Stuart Phillips). PhD candidate in Kinesiology. Podcast guest appearances.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Emerging researcher with genuine academic credentials at one of the world's leading exercise science labs (Stuart Phillips' group at McMaster). Specialises in resistance training for women, menopause-related changes, and evidence-based supplementation. Particularly valuable for ensuring the program's physical content is inclusive and informed on sex-specific considerations. Excellent science communicator on social media. No significant cautions — rigorous and balanced.

DOMAINS: Nutrition, Metabolism, Sugar, Processed Food, Neuroendocrinology
NOTABLE WORKS: Fat Chance: Beating the Odds Against Sugar, Processed Food, Obesity, and Disease, The Hacking of the American Mind, Metabolical: The Lure and the Lies of Processed Food, Nutrition, and Modern Medicine. Published Research (UCSF). Professor of Pediatric Endocrinology.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Legitimate researcher and pediatric endocrinologist at UCSF with genuine expertise in sugar metabolism and processed food. His 'Sugar: The Bitter Truth' lecture went viral. Often too much focus on mechanisms over outcomes, and tends toward single-cause explanations (fructose as the root of metabolic disease). Some commercial exposure. Useful for understanding processed food dangers (Week 12 evolutionary mismatch context), but balance with broader nutrition perspectives.

DOMAINS: Lipid Metabolism, Cardiovascular Health, Lipoprotein Science
NOTABLE WORKS: Lipid and Lipoprotein Disorders (with William Cromwell), Published Research. Frequent podcast guest (The Drive with Peter Attia, The Peter Attia Show). X @drlipid. Clinical lipidologist and Fellow of the National Lipid Association.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: One of the world's foremost clinical lipidologists. Unmatched depth on lipoprotein science and cardiovascular risk. Highly technical — best used as facilitator background knowledge for understanding heart health rather than participant-facing content. His Attia podcast appearances are masterclasses in lipid science. Relevant background for Week 10 (recovery, longevity context) and general health literacy. No significant cautions — impeccably evidence-based.

DOMAINS: Resistance Training, Protein, Muscle Physiology, Ageing, Women's Training, Exercise Science
NOTABLE WORKS: Instagram @mackinprof, Published Research (McMaster University). Professor of Kinesiology and Canada Research Chair in Skeletal Muscle Health. 400+ published papers.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: One of the most published and cited researchers in exercise science worldwide. Leading authority on protein requirements, resistance training adaptations, and muscle health across the lifespan. His lab at McMaster (where Lauren Colenso-Semple also works) is world-class. Directly relevant to Week 8 (strength training, progressive overload, protein) and the evidence base for resistance training benefiting everyone. Accessible on social media despite enormous academic credentials. No significant cautions — gold-standard researcher.

DOMAINS: Positive Psychology, Wellbeing, Optimism, Resilience, Character Strengths
NOTABLE WORKS: Flourish, Learned Optimism, Authentic Happiness, The Hope Circuit (memoir), Character Strengths and Virtues (with Christopher Peterson). Published Research (University of Pennsylvania). Former President of the American Psychological Association. Creator of PERMA model.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: The founder of positive psychology and creator of the PERMA model that structures this entire program. Learned Optimism is the basis for the pre-course and Week 16 optimism assessments. His character strengths work (with Peterson and the VIA Institute) underpins the VIA survey used from pre-course through Week 15. Former APA President with decades of rigorous published research. Foundational — the program cannot exist without this source. No significant cautions.

DOMAINS: Resistance Training, Nutrition, Behaviour Change, Motivation, Exercise Science, Coaching
NOTABLE WORKS: Certified Personal Trainer (CPT) certification, Transformation Specialist certification, Nutritionist certification, Exercise Therapy certification. International Sports Sciences Association.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Accredited certification body for personal training and fitness professionals. ISSA CPT and Transformation Specialist are the basis for the program creator's fitness and behaviour change credentials. Directly referenced throughout the Body phase (Weeks 7–9), Fuel & Recovery (Weeks 10–14), and behaviour change modules. Transformation Specialist certification is particularly relevant — it bridges exercise science with motivation and coaching. Curriculum is well-structured and evidence-informed for applied practice. No significant cautions.

DOMAINS: Aerobic Training, Exercise Physiology, Nutrition, Heart Rate Training, Endurance
NOTABLE WORKS: The Big Book of Endurance Training and Racing, The Endurance Handbook, The Big Book of Health and Fitness, The Maffetone Method, The Overfat Pandemic, 1:59 (on Eliud Kipchoge). Published Research. DC (Doctor of Chiropractic). Coached Mark Allen (six-time Ironman World Champion).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Pioneer of low-heart-rate aerobic training and the MAF (Maximum Aerobic Function) method. The 180-Age formula is directly taught in Week 9 as one practical approach for self-regulated aerobic training. The Maffetone Method is the preferred approach for beginners looking to start running or aerobic training. Coached some of the greatest endurance athletes in history. The revised course calendar frames MAF as 'one practical method' rather than the default, acknowledging that individualised HR zones may be more accurate for some — but the core principle that low-intensity aerobic work is foundational remains central to the program.

NOTABLE WORKS: Training for the Uphill Athlete (with Scott Johnston & Kilian Jornet), Training for the New Alpinism (with Scott Johnston)
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Respected alpinist and endurance coach. Practical, experience-based approach to aerobic development. Best used as applied coaching methodology alongside the physiology sources.

NOTABLE WORKS: You're Not Listening: What You're Missing and Why It Matters
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Well-researched journalism synthesising academic listening research. Excellent companion to the coaching and listening modules (Weeks 5–6). Not a primary researcher, but rigorous in sourcing.

NOTABLE WORKS: Breath: The New Science of a Lost Art
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Investigative journalist. Engaging and awareness-raising, but some claims (mouth-taping, nasal breathing protocols) are contested by ENT and sleep specialists. Good for sparking interest, not for prescriptive protocols.

NOTABLE WORKS: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind, Homo Deus, 21 Lessons for the 21st Century
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Popular historian-philosopher. Excellent for big-picture context on human evolution and behaviour. Not a specialist in any single domain — uses broad strokes that specialists sometimes dispute. Best for framing, not technical detail.

NOTABLE WORKS: Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us, To Sell Is Human, When: The Scientific Secrets of Perfect Timing, A Whole New Mind
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Good synthesiser of motivation research, particularly SDT. Drive is directly relevant to Week 2's motivation module. Populariser rather than primary researcher, but generally faithful to the science.

DOMAINS: Strategy, Human Nature, Power Dynamics, Leadership
NOTABLE WORKS: The Laws of Human Nature, Mastery, The 48 Laws of Power, The Art of Seduction, The 33 Strategies of War
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Thought-provoking but ethically complex. Historical anecdotes used selectively. The Laws of Human Nature is the most relevant to this program. Some works (48 Laws, Art of Seduction) promote manipulative tactics — use as perspective on human behaviour, not as coaching guidance.

NOTABLE WORKS: Creativity, Inc. (with Amy Wallace)
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Genuine leadership experience as co-founder of Pixar. Practical wisdom on psychological safety, creative collaboration, and honest feedback culture. Relevant to group dynamics and coaching environment.

NOTABLE WORKS: Thinking, Fast and Slow. Nobel Prize in Economics (2002). Published Research.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Nobel laureate. Foundational for understanding cognitive biases and dual-process thinking. Caution: some of the priming studies cited in the book have faced replication challenges, but the core System 1/System 2 framework remains robust and widely accepted.

NOTABLE WORKS: Nature Wants Us to Be Fat, The Fat Switch. Published Research (University of Colorado).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Legitimate researcher with an interesting fructose/uric acid hypothesis. Science is real but hypothesis-driven and not yet fully mainstream. Use for metabolic perspective, not as settled consensus.

NOTABLE WORKS: Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion, Pre-Suasion. Published Research (Arizona State University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Foundational work in persuasion psychology with well-replicated research. Relevant to coaching communication and understanding how influence operates in relationships. Classic and widely respected.

DOMAINS: Wellbeing, Lifestyle, Cold Water, Nordic Culture
NOTABLE WORKS: The Finnish Way: Finding Courage, Wellness, and Happiness Through the Power of Sisu
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Journalist and memoirist, not a researcher. Inspirational perspective on Nordic wellbeing culture, cold-water practices, and 'sisu' (Finnish resilience). Journalistic, not scientific — use for cultural enrichment and perspective.

NOTABLE WORKS: Burnout: The Secret to Unlocking the Stress Cycle. Emily Nagoski also authored Come As You Are.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Emily Nagoski has a PhD in Health Behavior. Evidence-based, particularly on the stress cycle and completing the stress response. Highly relevant to recovery, resilience, and the setbacks module (Week 11). Accessible and well-sourced.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Heart of Laser-Focused Coaching: A Revolutionary Approach to Masterful Coaching
NOTES and CAUTIONS: MCC (Master Certified Coach), ICF-credentialed. Respected coaching methodology with strong practical questioning frameworks. Directly relevant to coaching skill progression.

NOTABLE WORKS: Coach the Person, Not the Problem; The Discomfort Zone; Outsmart Your Brain
NOTES and CAUTIONS: PsyD, MCC, past Global President of ICF. Global authority on reflective inquiry and emotional connection in coaching. Strong on coaching presence and evoking awareness.

NOTABLE WORKS: Can't Hurt Me, Never Finished
NOTES and CAUTIONS: His story is inspiring and thought-provoking, but his approach is absolutely not for everyone, and toxic in some ways. Use with caution. The 'no excuses' ethos directly contradicts the program's 'Do What You Can' philosophy — useful as counterpoint, not model.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Peter Principle: Why Things Always Go Wrong (with Raymond Hull)
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Classic organisational theory — the Peter Principle (people rise to their level of incompetence) is widely accepted and cited. Useful for understanding workplace dynamics. Not core to the program but good contextual reference.

NOTABLE WORKS: Think and Grow Rich, Outwitting the Devil, The Law of Success
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Historically influential in the self-help canon, but extensively debunked. Fabricated many claims about his relationship with Andrew Carnegie. No evidence-based foundation. Use only for historical context — not credible as a coaching or psychology source.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck, Everything Is F*cked: A Book About Hope
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Popular philosophy with accessible writing. Not research-based but draws thoughtfully on stoicism and existentialism. Good for provoking reflection on values and meaning. Counter-cultural tone may resonate with participants resistant to typical self-help. Not a researcher.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Comfort Crisis, Scarcity Brain
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Health journalist, well-researched. Draws on evolutionary biology, anthropology, and adventure experience. Relevant to the 'Do What You Can' philosophy and the evolutionary mismatch framing in Weeks 7 and 12. Engaging writer.

NOTABLE WORKS: Man's Search for Meaning
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Psychiatrist, neurologist, and Holocaust survivor. Foundational work in logotherapy and meaning-making. Directly relevant to PERMA's 'M' (Meaning) and the program's meaning-focused philosophy. Essential reading on resilience and purpose.

NOTABLE WORKS: Change Your Questions, Change Your Life; Teaching That Changes Lives
NOTES and CAUTIONS: PhD, coaching consultant. Created the Judger/Learner framework for shifting mindset through self-inquiry. Pairs well with ICF competencies and open questioning skills (Week 5). Practical and directly applicable.

NOTABLE WORKS: Why We Get Sick: The Hidden Epidemic at the Root of Most Chronic Disease. Published Research (Brigham Young University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Legitimate PhD researcher but tends toward insulin-centric explanations for nearly everything. Useful for metabolic literacy and understanding insulin resistance, but not the full picture. Best used alongside broader nutrition sources.

DOMAINS: Psychiatry, Metabolism, Mental Health, Mitochondrial Function
NOTABLE WORKS: Brain Energy: A Revolutionary Breakthrough in Understanding Mental Health. Published Research (Harvard Medical School).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Harvard psychiatrist with legitimate research credentials. Novel metabolic theory of mental illness linking mitochondrial dysfunction to psychiatric conditions. Promising but still emerging — not yet mainstream consensus. Use with appropriate caveats about the theory's stage of development.

NOTABLE WORKS: What the Heck Should I Eat, The Blood Sugar Solution, Young Forever, Food Fix
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Some interesting principles, although some questionable assertions. Functional medicine lens brings commercial bias. Tends to overstate dietary interventions. Better as conversation-starter than authoritative source.

NOTABLE WORKS: Self-Compassion: The Proven Power of Being Kind to Yourself, Fierce Self-Compassion. Published Research (University of Texas at Austin).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Already referenced prominently in Week 11 (setbacks) and throughout the self-compassion thread. Primary researcher on self-compassion with robust published evidence. Three-component model (self-kindness, common humanity, mindfulness) is well-validated. Essential source for this program.

NOTABLE WORKS: Nonviolent Communication: A Language of Life
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Creator of NVC (Nonviolent Communication). Directly referenced in Week 6 as a primary framework. Foundational for the program's communication module. NVC is widely used in coaching, mediation, and education worldwide.

NOTABLE WORKS: How Emotions Are Made: The Secret Life of the Brain, Seven and a Half Lessons About the Brain. Published Research (Northeastern University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Leading researcher on constructed emotion theory and emotional granularity — already a core reference in Week 6. Her work on interoception is also referenced in Week 14. Rigorous, sometimes controversial among traditional emotion researchers, but well-supported.

NOTABLE WORKS: Huberman Lab Podcast. Published Research (Stanford University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Referenced in course for RAS (Week 3) and sleep protocols (Week 10). Legitimate Stanford neuroscientist, but recent personal controversies and a tendency to make strong protocol recommendations from limited evidence. Some supplement and protocol claims exceed the research. Use the peer-reviewed work, not the podcast opinions.

NOTABLE WORKS: Never Split the Difference: Negotiating as if Your Life Depended on It
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Former FBI lead hostage negotiator. Tactical empathy, mirroring, and labelling techniques are woven into the coaching skill progression (Weeks 6–8). Practical and effective techniques, though originally designed for high-stakes negotiation — adapt the tone for coaching context.

NOTABLE WORKS: Permission to Feel: Unlocking the Power of Emotions to Help Our Kids, Ourselves, and Our Society Thrive. Published Research (Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Director of the Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence. Referenced in Week 6 (emotion naming). Created the RULER framework for emotional literacy. Rigorous researcher with practical educational applications. Highly relevant to emotional granularity work.

DOMAINS: Stages of Change, Behavior Change, Psychology
NOTABLE WORKS: Changing for Good (Prochaska). Published Research (Transtheoretical Model).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Creators of the Transtheoretical Model (Stages of Change) — core to Week 4 and revisited in Week 11. One of the most influential behavior change frameworks in health psychology. Well-validated across multiple domains.

NOTABLE WORKS: Intrinsic Motivation and Self-Determination in Human Behavior, Why We Do What We Do (Deci). Published Research (University of Rochester).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Creators of Self-Determination Theory — core to Week 2 (motivation). Autonomy, competence, and relatedness framework is one of the most robust motivation theories in psychology. Extensively validated.

NOTABLE WORKS: Tiny Habits: The Small Changes That Change Everything. Published Research (Stanford University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Stanford behavior scientist. Tiny Habits method aligns closely with the program's 'too small to fail' micro-win philosophy. Not currently referenced in the course but a natural complement to Clear (Atomic Habits) and Wood (Good Habits, Bad Habits). Strong research foundation.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Happiness Trap, The Confidence Gap, ACT Made Simple
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Medical practitioner and ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy) trainer. Strong on values clarification and psychological flexibility. Relevant to the values work in Weeks 1, 15, and throughout. Practical, evidence-informed, accessible.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Creative Act: A Way of Being
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Legendary music producer (Johnny Cash, Beastie Boys, Red Hot Chili Peppers, Adele). The Creative Act is not about music — it's a philosophy of awareness, presence, and trusting your own process. Deeply aligned with the program's coaching ethos: listen before you act, trust what's emerging, stay open. Relevant to presence (Week 5), values (Weeks 1 & 15), and the 'Do What You Can' philosophy throughout. Not a researcher — a practitioner with extraordinary range.

NOTABLE WORKS: Emotional Agility: Get Unstuck, Embrace Change, and Thrive in Work and Life. Published Research (Harvard Medical School).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Harvard psychologist. Emotional agility — the ability to navigate emotions flexibly rather than suppressing or being consumed by them — is directly relevant to emotional granularity (Week 6), setbacks (Week 11), and the entire self-compassion thread. TED talk has 10M+ views. Complements Barrett and Brackett.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Willpower Instinct, The Upside of Stress, The Joy of Movement. Published Research (Stanford University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Stanford health psychologist. The Joy of Movement makes a compelling case that movement is fundamentally tied to joy, social connection, and meaning — perfectly aligned with the Body phase (Weeks 7–9). The Upside of Stress reframes stress as potentially growth-promoting. Useful counterpoint to stress-is-toxic narratives.

NOTABLE WORKS: When the Body Says No, In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts, The Myth of Normal (with Daniel Maté)
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Physician with deep clinical experience in addiction and trauma. Powerful perspective on how emotional patterns manifest physically. Relevant to emotional eating (Week 14) and self-compassion. Caution: sometimes overstates causal claims and strays beyond evidence. Best used for perspective and compassion framing, not clinical authority.

NOTABLE WORKS: Why We Sleep: Unlocking the Power of Sleep and Dreams. Published Research (UC Berkeley).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Leading sleep researcher with legitimate credentials. Why We Sleep was hugely influential but has been criticised for overstatement, data errors, and alarmist framing (notably by Alexey Guzey's detailed critique). The core message — sleep matters enormously — is solid. Use the principles, not the specific statistics. Relevant to Week 10.

NOTABLE WORKS: Grit: The Power of Passion and Perseverance. Published Research (University of Pennsylvania).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: MacArthur Fellow and professor of psychology. Grit research is well-known though debated — critics argue it overlaps substantially with conscientiousness and underweights systemic barriers. Relevant to the motivation and persistence threads but use alongside the program's compassion-first approach, not as 'just push harder' framing.

NOTABLE WORKS: Full Catastrophe Living, Wherever You Go There You Are. Published Research (UMass Medical School).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Creator of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR). Foundational figure in bringing mindfulness into medicine and mainstream wellbeing. Relevant to mindful eating (Week 14), recovery (Week 10), self-compassion, and presence throughout the coaching modules. Extensively validated clinical outcomes.

NOTABLE WORKS: Radical Acceptance, Radical Compassion, True Refuge. PhD in Clinical Psychology.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Clinical psychologist and meditation teacher. RAIN framework (Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture) is a practical self-compassion tool. Complements Kristin Neff's work and is applicable to the setbacks module (Week 11) and emotional relationship with food (Week 14). Bridges contemplative practice and clinical psychology.

DOMAINS: Polyvagal Theory, Nervous System, Safety, Co-Regulation
NOTABLE WORKS: The Polyvagal Theory, The Pocket Guide to the Polyvagal Theory. Published Research (Indiana University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Creator of Polyvagal Theory — influential in understanding how the nervous system mediates safety, connection, and threat responses. Relevant to psychological safety (Week 1), coaching presence, and stress-recovery (Week 10). Caution: Polyvagal Theory is widely used in therapy circles but has faced scientific criticism for oversimplifying neuroscience. Use the practical wisdom about safety and co-regulation, not the specific neural pathway claims.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Published Research (Boston University).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Pioneering trauma researcher. The Body Keeps the Score is enormously influential on understanding how the body holds stress and emotional experience. Relevant to movement foundations (Week 7) and embodiment throughout. Caution: some institutional controversies, and the book can lead participants toward self-diagnosis. The program is not therapy — reference the body-awareness principles, not trauma treatment.

NOTABLE WORKS: Mindsight, The Whole-Brain Child, Aware. Published Research (UCLA).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Clinical professor of psychiatry at UCLA. Created the 'Window of Tolerance' concept and interpersonal neurobiology framework. Relevant to emotional regulation, coaching presence, and understanding how stress affects capacity. Practical and well-regarded in coaching and therapy circles.

NOTABLE WORKS: Multipliers: How the Best Leaders Make Everyone Smarter, Impact Players, Rookie Smarts
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Leadership researcher. Multipliers framework — how some leaders amplify the intelligence around them while others diminish it — is directly applicable to peer coaching dynamics and group facilitation. Relevant to the coaching environment and how participants show up for each other.

NOTABLE WORKS: The Obstacle Is the Way, Ego Is the Enemy, Stillness Is the Key, The Daily Stoic, Discipline Is Destiny
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Populariser of Stoic philosophy with accessible, practical writing. The Obstacle Is the Way reframes adversity as opportunity — relevant to setbacks (Week 11). Stillness Is the Key connects to presence and recovery. Not a philosopher or researcher, but faithful to the source material and practically useful.

DOMAINS: Personal Effectiveness, Leadership, Values, Habits, Communication, Self-Renewal
NOTABLE WORKS: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People (40M+ copies sold), First Things First, The 8th Habit, Principle-Centered Leadership. Co-founder of FranklinCovey.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: One of the most influential personal development authors of the 20th century. Several habits map directly to this program: 'Begin with the end in mind' (values and identity-based goals, Weeks 1 & 15), 'Seek first to understand, then to be understood' (coaching, listening, NVC, Weeks 5–6), 'Sharpen the saw' (recovery, balance, self-renewal, Weeks 10 & 15–16), and 'Circle of Influence vs. Circle of Concern' (setbacks, Week 11). Inside-out approach (character ethic over personality ethic) aligns with values-led coaching. Not a primary researcher but draws on well-established psychological principles (Frankl, Maslow, Drucker). Some religious/conservative underpinning (Covey was LDS) that occasionally surfaces, but the core work is secular and universal. Later editions and the FranklinCovey organisation have become commercially driven — the original work is stronger. Can feel prescriptive compared to the program's 'Do What You Can' flexibility. Best used selectively — specific habits as reference points rather than a wholesale framework.

DOMAINS: Diabetes Remission, Metabolism, Nutrition, Weight Management, Metabolic Health
NOTABLE WORKS: Life Without Diabetes, Your Simple Guide to Reversing Type 2 Diabetes. Published Research (Newcastle University). Led the landmark DiRECT trial (Diabetes Remission Clinical Trial).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Professor of Medicine and Metabolism at Newcastle University. Led the first rigorous clinical trial demonstrating that Type 2 diabetes can be put into remission through dietary intervention — fundamentally shifting the medical establishment's view from 'progressive and irreversible' to 'potentially remissible'. Decades of published peer-reviewed research. Relevant to Weeks 12–13 (nutrition foundations, metabolic flexibility, evaluating dietary approaches). Provides the credible, evidence-based metabolic health perspective that popular sources like Inchauspé oversimplify. No significant cautions — primary researcher with clinical trial evidence.

DOMAINS: Glucose Metabolism, Nutrition, Meal Timing, Diabetes Management, Dietetics
NOTABLE WORKS: Published Research (University of Westminster, previously Oxford and King's College London). PhD in Nutrition and Dietetics (King's College London). Registered Dietitian. Active science communicator on social media.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: Senior Lecturer and registered dietitian specialising in glucose metabolism, meal timing, and dietary management of diabetes. Publishes the actual peer-reviewed research behind claims that popular influencers (e.g., 'Glucose Goddess') oversimplify — food order effects, meal timing, glucose response in healthy vs. diabetic populations. Active in debunking glucose misinformation with evidence rather than hype. Relevant to Weeks 12–13 (nutrition foundations, dietary approaches) and metabolic flexibility content. Maintains the program's ethos: evidence over fear, nourishment over restriction. No significant cautions — rigorous researcher and registered clinician.

DOMAINS: Nutrition, Evidence-Based Health, Research Interpretation, Diet Evaluation
NOTABLE WORKS: Nutrition Made Simple (YouTube channel). MD and PhD in Molecular Biology (UCLA).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: MD/PhD with genuine academic credentials. Nutrition Made Simple is one of the most balanced and methodical nutrition channels available — walks through actual studies, examines methodology, and presents conclusions honestly without ideological bias. Models exactly the critical thinking skill that Week 13's dietary evaluation framework teaches: assess sustainability, nutrient adequacy, personal fit, and evidence base. Calm, non-tribal, and non-commercial — no supplements, programmes, or diets to sell. Not a primary nutrition researcher (PhD is in molecular biology), which keeps him from VERY HIGH, but his skill in honest research interpretation is exceptional. Relevant to Weeks 12–13 as accessible video content that complements the deeper academic sources (Taylor, Guess, Phillips). No significant cautions.

DOMAINS: Exercise Physiology, Resistance Training, Nutrition, Supplementation, Cardiovascular Health, Longevity, Sleep
NOTABLE WORKS: Physionic (YouTube channel). PhD in Exercise Physiology/Kinesiology.
NOTES and CAUTIONS: PhD in exercise science with a methodical, evidence-based approach to health and fitness research. Physionic covers a broader range than most science communicators — resistance training, cardiovascular health, nutrition, sleep, supplementation, and longevity — always grounded in multiple studies rather than single-paper cherry-picking. Calm, measured presentation with no hype or clickbait. Non-commercial — focused on education over products. Natural complement to Gil Carvalho (Nutrition Made Simple): together they cover the nutrition and exercise/health domains with the same honest, non-tribal methodology. Relevant across the Body phase (Weeks 7–9), recovery (Week 10), and nutrition (Weeks 12–13). Useful as facilitator preparation or recommended participant viewing. No significant cautions.

DOMAINS: Humanistic Psychology, Self-Actualization, Creativity, Wellbeing, Intelligence, Human Potential, Positive Psychology
NOTABLE WORKS: Transcend: The New Science of Self-Actualization (2020). Wired to Create (with Carolyn Gregoire, 2015). Ungifted: Intelligence Redefined (2013). The Psychology Podcast (700+ episodes).
NOTES and CAUTIONS: PhD in cognitive psychology from Yale; trained under Martin Seligman at the University of Pennsylvania's Positive Psychology Center. The foremost modern interpreter of Abraham Maslow's work — Transcend revisits Maslow's original writings (including unpublished journals) and updates the hierarchy with contemporary neuroscience, personality psychology, and wellbeing research. Replaces the rigid pyramid (which Maslow never actually drew) with a sailboat metaphor: a hull of security, connection, and self-esteem that keeps you afloat, and a sail of exploration, love, and purpose that moves you forward. This reframing aligns perfectly with the program's 'Do What You Can' philosophy — growth as an ongoing, non-linear process rather than a ladder to climb. Also brings rigorous creativity research (Wired to Create) and a compassionate rethinking of intelligence and giftedness (Ungifted). The Psychology Podcast is one of the most intellectually honest long-form interview shows in the space, featuring leading researchers across psychology, neuroscience, and philosophy. No significant cautions — occasionally speculative in podcast conversations, but always clearly signals when he is hypothesising vs citing evidence. Relevant to Weeks 1–2 (identity, values, self-awareness), Week 15 (integration and growth), and the overarching program philosophy of whole-person development.
